_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Kandiyohi County, MN. Record survival, courthouse history — county depopulated in 1862, railroad resettlement, Willmar as Great Northern division point.

previous_courthouses.fate: County depopulated during US-Dakota War. Settlers and government functions abandoned.
County seat moved to Willmar in 1870 when the railroad made Willmar the dominant town.
Replaced by 1965 modern building. Records transferred.

record_losses.description: Kandiyohi County was on the active frontier of the US-Dakota War. Multiple settler families were killed in the initial attacks of August 1862 (the Broberg and Lundborg homesteads). The entire non-Dakota population fled within days, and the county was essentially uninhabited by Euro-Americans for two to three years. The original county seat at Kandiyohi village was abandoned, and records left behind in unattended buildings suffered damage from weather, animals, and neglect. When the county was reorganized in the mid-1860s, the surviving records were fragmentary. The practical effect is that Kandiyohi County's documentary record begins effectively in 1865-1866 for most purposes.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name}'s family was in Kandiyohi County before August 1862, the early records may not survive. The damage claims filed after the war and the federal census of 1860 may be the only documentation of their pre-war presence.
record_losses.source: Lawson, Victor E. History of Kandiyohi County, Minnesota. St. Paul, 1905. Carley, Kenneth. The Dakota War of 1862. 2nd ed. St. Paul: Minnesota Historical Society Press, 1976.

deeds.completeness: Good from resettlement onward. Pre-1862 land records are incomplete — some early claims were re-registered after resettlement.
deeds.gaps: Significant gap from county organization (1858) through 1865. Federal land office records (General Land Office) at the National Archives are the best source for early claims.
deeds.repository: Kandiyohi County Recorder, Willmar; Minnesota Historical Society microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com MN land records; BLM GLO online records

wills_estates.completeness: Good from the late 1860s onward. No pre-war probate records survive.
wills_estates.gaps: The county's pre-war population was small and few estates were probated before 1862.
wills_estates.repository: Kandiyohi County Probate Court, Willmar; Minnesota Historical Society

marriages.completeness: Good from resettlement. No pre-war civil marriage records survive.
marriages.gaps: Swedish and Norwegian church marriages were sometimes the only record, especially 1865–1880.
marriages.repository: Kandiyohi County Recorder; Minnesota Historical Society microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Good from reorganization of county government
court_minutes.gaps: No court records from the 1858–1865 period survive.
court_minutes.repository: Kandiyohi County Courthouse; Minnesota Historical Society

vital_records.completeness: Compliance was slow in rural Kandiyohi County until the 1890s. Swedish Lutheran kyrkobok records are more complete for the 1870–1895 period.
vital_records.repository: Minnesota Department of Health; Kandiyohi County Recorder; Minnesota Historical Society

naturalization_records.completeness: Good — the wave of Swedish immigrants recruited by the railroad generated substantial naturalization filings from the late 1860s onward
naturalization_records.repository: Kandiyohi County District Court; National Archives — Chicago; Minnesota Historical Society

substitute_sources.description: Swedish Augustana Synod congregations organized rapidly after resettlement. Vinje Lutheran (Willmar), Mamre, and others maintained Swedish-language parish registers with communion books (husforhorslaengder) that track family presence year by year.
substitute_sources.repository: Swenson Swedish Immigration Research Center, Rock Island, IL; Augustana Heritage Association; individual congregations; FamilySearch microfilm
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Swedish kyrkobok records are exceptionally detailed — communion books track every family member's attendance, movement in and out of the parish, and literacy. These records often note the immigrant's home parish in Sweden. For families arriving through the Great Northern Railway's Swedish recruitment, church records may document the chain of migration.
substitute_sources.source: Ander, O. Fritiof. The Cultural Heritage of the Swedish Immigrant. Rock Island, IL: Augustana Historical Society, 1956.

substitute_sources.description: The 1860 federal census captures Kandiyohi County's pre-war population — a critical record because so many other pre-war documents were lost. The 1870 census reflects the resettlement generation. Minnesota state censuses (1865, 1875, 1885, 1895, 1905) provide additional data.
substitute_sources.repository: National Archives; FamilySearch; Ancestry.com; Minnesota Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The 1860 census is irreplaceable for pre-war Kandiyohi County — it may be the only surviving record of families who were killed or who fled and never returned.
substitute_sources.source: Minnesota Historical Society. Minnesota Census Records Guide. St. Paul: MHS, 2004.

substitute_sources.description: Federal land patents for homestead, pre-emption, and cash entry claims. The GLO records document the original transfer of land from the federal government to private ownership.
substitute_sources.repository: Bureau of Land Management GLO Records (online at glorecords.blm.gov); National Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Because Kandiyohi County's early deed books are incomplete, the federal GLO patent records may be the only documentation of {ancestor_name}'s original land claim. These records are fully digitized and freely available online.
substitute_sources.source: Bureau of Land Management. General Land Office Records. https://glorecords.blm.gov

substitute_sources.description: Settler families who lost property during the 1862 war filed claims with the state of Minnesota, documenting specific losses — buildings, livestock, crops, personal property.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Damage claims provide detailed inventories of what settlers owned at the time of the war — the most complete snapshot of pre-war Kandiyohi County farm life that survives.
substitute_sources.source: Minnesota Historical Society. Claims for Depredations by Sioux Indians. St. Paul: MHS collections.

narrative_hooks: Kandiyohi County's pre-war records were largely lost when the county was abandoned in 1862 — if {ancestor_name} was here before the war, the 1860 census and the federal land patent may be the only surviving documentation of {pronoun_possessive} presence.

The Swedish Lutheran church records that begin in 1866 are Kandiyohi County's most complete genealogical resource for the resettlement generation — more detailed than the civil records and written in the language the families actually spoke.

The federal General Land Office records are freely available online and may document {ancestor_name}'s homestead claim even though the county deed books for that period are lost — the federal system preserved what the county system could not.

If {ancestor_name} filed a damage claim after the 1862 war, that claim may be the most detailed surviving record of {pronoun_possessive} pre-war life in Kandiyohi County — listing every cow, every acre broken, every building erected.
source_references: Lawson, Victor E. History of Kandiyohi County, Minnesota. St. Paul, 1905.
Carley, Kenneth. The Dakota War of 1862. 2nd ed. St. Paul: Minnesota Historical Society Press, 1976.
Ander, O. Fritiof. The Cultural Heritage of the Swedish Immigrant. Rock Island, IL: Augustana Historical Society, 1956.
Minnesota Historical Society. Minnesota Census Records Guide. St. Paul: MHS, 2004.
Bureau of Land Management. General Land Office Records. https://glorecords.blm.gov
